Rare, Overlooked, or Underappreciated Causes of Recurrent Abdominal Pain: A Primer for Gastroenterologists
  • Source: Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology 2023;21:264–279
  • Topic: Recurrent abdominal pain – rare causes, diagnosis, and management
  • Author(s): Brenner DM, Brandt LJ, Fenster M, Hamilton MJ, Kamboj AK, Oxentenko AS, Wang B, Chey WD
Recurrent Abdominal Pain Overview
  • Definition: pain persisting >3 months, occurring intermittently
  • 19.4 million total US visits in 2016 (6.9M office + 12.5M ED)
  • Annual US healthcare expenditure: $9.5 billion (2018)
  • >50% of repeat ED visits for abdominal pain are high utilizers
  • Standard workup often inconclusive → exploratory surgery not uncommon
1. Chronic Abdominal Wall Pain (CAWP)
1.1 Disease Background
  • Also known as anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome
  • Underlies 10%–30% of undifferentiated abdominal pain in outpatient setting
  • Diagnosis often delayed after extensive testing
  • Women:men ratio 4:1
  • More common in obesity, pain syndrome comorbidities, and depression
1.2 Pathophysiology
  • Entrapment of cutaneous branches of thoracic nerves passing through rectus abdominis muscle
1.3 Clinical Features
  • Pain typically focal and positional
  • Right upper quadrant most common site
  • May have associated allodynia or hyperalgesia
  • Often present over prior surgical scar (absence doesn't rule out)
  • Localizable with one finger
  • Worsens with actions that tense abdominal muscles:
  • Bending, sitting, lying on affected side
  • Coughing, sneezing
  • Red flags requiring other consideration: persistent vomiting, melena, sudden bowel pattern change, unintentional weight loss
1.4 Diagnosis
  • Carnett's sign: pain worse with muscle tension (raise legs or chin-to-chest)
  • Pinch test: disproportionate intense pain when pinching tender area vs contralateral side
  • Improvement following trigger point injection (TPI) supports diagnosis
1.5 Management Options
  • Mild symptoms: lidocaine patch, heat or ice (personal preference)
  • Moderate–severe: TPI with lidocaine ± glucocorticoid (betamethasone/triamcinolone)
  • Lidocaine relief starts few hours post-administration
  • Glucocorticoid takes several days to take effect
  • Repeat TPI no sooner than 3 months if incomplete response or recurrence
  • Refractory cases: chemical neurolysis or surgical neurectomy
2. Vascular Causes of Recurrent Abdominal Pain
2.1 Overview
  • Includes: MALS, CMI, SMVT, splanchnic artery aneurysms
  • True incidence and prevalence unknown due to frequent misdiagnosis
2.2 Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome (MALS)
2.2.1 Background
  • Also called celiac artery compression syndrome
  • Women:men ratio 4:1, typically thin middle-aged women
  • No universally accepted definition
2.2.2 Clinical Features
  • Postprandial epigastric pain (up to 90% of patients)
  • Weight loss
  • Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
  • Abdominal bruit intensifying on deep expiration
2.2.3 Diagnosis
  • Diagnosis of exclusion
  • Initial workup: EGD, RUQ ultrasound, CT abdomen/pelvis
  • If negative → CTA or MRA with deep expiration (shows characteristic hooking)
  • Duplex ultrasound with inspiratory/expiratory measurements also useful
2.2.4 Management
  • Operative management if symptomatic with confirmed compression on imaging
  • Surgical options: open or laparoscopic (standard/robotic)
  • Often concurrent celiac ganglionectomy
  • Systematic review of 400 patients:
  • Laparoscopic → 96% immediate relief vs 78% open
  • Late recurrence similar (5.7% vs 6.8%)
  • CT/endoscopic ultrasound–guided celiac ganglion blockade as alternative
2.3 Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia (CMI)
2.3.1 Background
  • <5% of all ischemic intestinal disorders
  • Dutch prospective study: incidence 9.2 per 100,000 inhabitants
  • Atherosclerotic CMI: 7.2 per 100,000
  • MALS-related: 1.3 per 100,000
  • Nonocclusive: 0.6 per 100,000
  • Women > men (12.0 vs 6.5 per 100,000)
  • Incidence ↑ with age
  • Average diagnosis time: 10–15 months from symptom onset
2.3.2 Pathophysiology
  • Results from transient inadequate intestinal blood flow during digestion
  • Most often from mesenteric atherosclerosis
  • Can also result from: vasculitis, MALS, fibromuscular dysplasia
  • Nonocclusive types seen in low-flow states: heart failure, COPD, chronic kidney disease
  • 18%–29% of elderly have mesenteric artery stenosis
  • Most symptomatic CMI patients have ≥2 vessel involvement (91%)
  • Single-vessel disease usually requires >70% stenosis, typically in celiac artery (86%)
2.3.3 Clinical Features
  • Classic triad: postprandial pain, weight loss, abdominal bruit (22% of patients)
  • Abdominal discomfort/cramping within 30 minutes of eating
  • Pain resolves slowly over 1–4 hours
  • Progressive worsening: mild → continuous (indicates impending intestinal infarction)
  • Weight loss (61%–78%, mean 10.4 ± 5.4 kg)
  • Nausea/vomiting (13%), diarrhea (7%–13%), lower GI bleeding (8%)
  • Physical exam typically unremarkable, abdomen soft and non-tender
2.3.4 Diagnosis
  • CTA is imaging modality of choice
  • Sensitivity 96%, specificity 94% for mesenteric vascular occlusion
  • MRA: no radiation but less accurate than CTA in small studies
  • Mesenteric angiography → gold standard historically, now limited role
  • Duplex ultrasonography: operator-dependent, less accurate than CTA
  • High-grade stenosis (≥70% occlusion) in ≥2 vessels typically diagnostic
  • Endoscopy: only nonspecific findings (erythema 42%, edema 35%)
  • Functional duplex ultrasonography: portal vein flow fasting vs postprandial
  • Lab tests: postprandial D-dimer and lactate significantly elevated
2.3.5 Management
  • Surgical or endovascular revascularization
  • Meta-analysis comparison:
  • Endovascular → fewer postoperative complications (RR 2.19)
  • Surgical → trend toward improved 30-day mortality + superior long-term outcomes
  • Recent guidelines: endovascular preferred for most patients (short-term mortality benefit)
  • Younger/longer life expectancy → consider surgery first-line
  • Success rates: 85%–100% endovascular, 97%–100% surgical
  • Immediate relief: 87%–95% endovascular, 90%–98% surgical
  • 5-year relief maintenance: 51% endovascular, 89%–92% surgical
2.4 Subacute Mesenteric Venous Thrombosis (SMVT)
2.4.1 Background
  • Incidence: 2.0–2.7 per 100,000 person-years
  • 24%–40% of MVT are chronic/subacute
  • Peak incidence: 70–79 years (11.3 per 100,000 person-years)
2.4.2 Pathophysiology
  • Caused by: hypercoagulability, blood flow stagnation, endothelial damage
  • Most common sites: portal, superior mesenteric, splenic veins
  • Subacute IMV thrombosis is rare
2.4.3 Clinical Features
  • "Tumbleweed" pain pattern: relapsing and remitting nature
  • Due to recurrent thromboses + collateral vessel development
2.4.4 Diagnosis
  • Physical exam and labs most often normal
  • Requires high index of suspicion
  • CTA is imaging modality of choice (sensitivity 93%, specificity 100%)
  • Magnetic resonance venography also highly accurate
  • Diagnostic: occlusion of mesenteric veins with collateral compensation
2.4.5 Management
  • Conservative: systemic anticoagulation for 3–6 months
  • Prevents further extension, allows recanalization
  • Surgical exploration reserved for bowel infarction signs
3. Allergic Causes of Recurrent Abdominal Pain
3.1 Overview
  • Consider in patients with atopy history (eczema, allergic rhinitis, asthma)
  • Family history of atopy important clue
3.2 Pathophysiology
  • IgE-mediated allergy: allergen crosslinks IgE on mast cell surface → degranulation
  • Non-IgE mechanisms can also activate mast cells
  • Hereditary angioedema (HAE): deficient C1 inhibitor → overproduction of bradykinin + unopposed C4 breakdown
3.3 Food Allergy
3.3.1 Clinical Features
  • GI symptoms: diffuse crampy pain, bloating, nausea, loose stools
  • Predictable symptoms immediately after ingestion → resolve within 1 day
  • Most common adult allergens: shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts
3.3.2 Diagnosis
  • Food-specific IgE blood and skin testing
3.3.3 Management
  • Strict avoidance of culprit foods
  • Always have nonexpired epinephrine pen available
3.4 Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS)
3.4.1 Clinical Features
  • Skin: flushing, hives, pruritus
  • Airway: throat scratching, chest tightness, rhinitis
  • GI: abdominal bloat and cramping pain
  • Pain not isolated → coexists with skin/other mast cell manifestations
  • Symptoms: intermittent, variable duration (hours to days)
  • Classic triggers:
  • Alcohol
  • Gluten, processed foods with dyes/chemicals
  • Medications (antibiotics)
  • Strong smells (fragrances, cleaning products)
3.4.2 Diagnosis
  • Baseline serum tryptase
  • >20 ng/mL → further workup for systemic mastocytosis
  • >8 ng/mL suggests hereditary alpha-tryptasemia
  • Repeat tryptase immediately after severe reaction (within 2–6 hours)
  • Positive if ≥20% + 2 ng/mL above baseline
  • 24-hour urine collection when symptomatic for:
  • Histamine (N-methylhistamine)
  • Prostaglandin-F2 alpha or D2
  • Leukotriene-E4
3.4.3 Management
  • Diagnosis supported by clinical response to mast cell/mast cell mediator blockers
  • First-line: nonsedating H1 antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine) at symptom onset
  • If partial relief → add mast cell stabilizing medications, titrate based on response
3.5 Angioedema and Hereditary Angioedema (HAE)
3.5.1 Clinical Features
  • Swelling of skin (face, limbs, trunk) and mucosal surfaces including GI tract
  • Intestinal angioedema: crampy pain ± nausea, distension, diarrhea
  • Severity variable → can prompt ED visits and unnecessary surgeries if unrecognized
  • HAE: sudden-onset crampy diffuse pain lasting several days
  • No hives, no obvious exposure
  • 50% of HAE patients experience life-threatening laryngeal edema
  • Inheritance: autosomal dominant → family history of angioedema
3.5.2 Diagnosis
  • CT scan during active symptoms may show:
  • Segmental bowel with straightening, wall thickening, mild dilation
  • Features not always present → history and physical often only clues
  • HAE screening: complement C4 test (low even when asymptomatic)
  • Typically <50% of normal in HAE patients
  • If suspected → next test: C1 inhibitor level and function to confirm + determine subtype
3.5.3 Management
  • Food-mediated allergies: strict avoidance + epinephrine pen
  • MCAS: H1 antihistamines → add mast cell stabilizers if partial response
  • HAE: refer to allergy/immunology specialist
  • Antihistamines (IV diphenhydramine) ineffective in acute setting
  • Requires: recombinant C1 inhibitor or bradykinin antagonists
4. Acute Hepatic Porphyria (AHP)
4.1 Disease Background
  • Inherited disorders of heme biosynthesis pathway
  • 8 enzymatic steps → 8 inherited porphyrias
  • 4 acute hepatic porphyrias (AHP):
  • Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP)
  • Variegate porphyria (VP)
  • Hereditary coproporphyria (HCP)
  • 5-ALA dehydratase deficiency porphyria (very rare, <10 cases worldwide)
  • ~90% of symptomatic AHP patients are women in reproductive years
  • Most common presentation: episodic severe abdominal pain
  • Average time from symptom onset to diagnosis: 15 years
4.2 Pathophysiology
  • Heme synthesis controlled by 5-ALAS1 (rate-limiting enzyme)
  • In AHPs, defective downstream enzyme becomes rate-limiting
  • Results in accumulation of ALA and PBG
  • Insufficient heme → further activates ALAS1 → acute attack setting
  • ALA is a neurotoxin
  • Porphyrins act as photosensitizers → skin damage
  • AIP, HCP, VP are autosomal dominant
  • Genetic carrier prevalence: 1:1300 to 1:1785 (much higher than thought)
  • Estimated penetrance of symptomatic disease: ~1% of AIP gene carriers
  • Low penetrance → additional factors required for symptomatic manifestation
4.3 Precipitating Factors
  • Progesterone (especially)
  • Medications/chemicals inducing cytochrome P450
  • Acute illness (infection)
  • Stress
  • Excess alcohol intake
  • Caloric deprivation
4.4 Clinical Features (All AHPs)
  • 95% present with severe nonfocal abdominal pain during attacks
  • Associated: nausea, vomiting, constipation
  • Neurologic symptoms present in up to 70% of acute attacks
  • All nervous system divisions can be affected
  • Progression variable → can rapidly progress to flaccid tetraplegia, respiratory paralysis within days
  • Prolonged/frequent attacks → permanent neurologic damage, neuropathy, chronic pain
4.5 Recurrent Attacks
  • Vast majority experience only one or few attacks in lifetime
  • ~3%–5% of symptomatic AHP patients experience recurrent attacks (>4/year)
  • Usually no identifiable triggers
  • >50% of frequent-recurrence patients have chronic neurologic symptoms
  • 35% have diagnosis of neuropathy
  • Markedly impaired quality of life
  • Higher risk for: liver disease, hepatocellular carcinoma, chronic renal failure
  • ALA/PBG elevated even between attacks in frequent-recurrence patients
4.6 Diagnosis
  • Single spot urine test for ALA, PBG, and creatinine during or within few days of acute attack
  • Diagnostic if: >4× upper limit of normal for ALA/PBG, or urine PBG >10 mg/g creatinine
  • After acute AIP attack: ALA and PBG can remain elevated for weeks to months
  • Genetic testing used for confirmation, not first-line (low penetrance)
4.7 Management
  • During acute attack:
  • Stop identifiable precipitating factors (P450-inducing medications)
  • Carbohydrate loading in early stages
  • Definitive treatment: intravenous heme infusions
  • Heme mechanism: rapidly downregulates ALAS1 → stops ALA/PBG accumulation
  • Symptomatic relief depends on ALA/PBG elimination → takes 48–72 hours
  • Timely heme treatment:
  • Normalizes ALA/PBG levels
  • Improves attack symptoms
  • Avoids long-term neurologic symptoms
4.8 Prevention in Frequent Attacks
  • Givosiran: ALAS1-specific siRNA administered subcutaneously monthly
  • Hepatocyte-selective uptake
  • Effectively normalizes ALA levels
  • Significantly reduces acute attacks
5. Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF)
5.1 Disease Background
  • Oldest described, most common autoinflammatory periodic fever syndrome
  • Origins believed to date to early Mesopotamia
  • Most common in Mediterranean descent populations
  • In US: primarily Ashkenazi Jewish and Middle Eastern populations
  • Documented >100,000 cases globally (limited epidemiological data)
  • Men:women 1.2:1
  • >90% experience first episode before 20 years of age
  • Onset after 40 rare → usually milder disease
5.2 Pathophysiology
  • Historically autosomal recessive, now recognized as more complex
  • MEFV gene on chromosome 16 encodes pyrin (found in myeloid cell cytoplasm)
  • >300 mutations described; 5 common ones account for >66% of cases:
  • M694V, M680I, V726A, M694I, E148Q
  • M694V most pathogenic → homozygous patients have more severe course
  • 10%–20% meeting diagnostic criteria have no identifiable MEFV mutations
  • Up to 25% carry one or none of known mutations
  • Updated hypothesis: FMF phenotype may be autosomal dominant
  • Mutations cause gain-of-function in pyrin → inflammation via IL-1, IL-18, and other chemotactic agents
5.3 Clinical Features
  • Typical attacks last 1–4 days, remit spontaneously
  • Patients symptom-free between attacks
  • Identified triggers: infections, surgery, menstruation, cold exposure, emotional stress, vigorous exercise
  • Some experience prodromes: constitutional symptoms, mild discomfort at inflammation sites, anxiety, taste/appetite changes
5.3.1 Classic Episode Features
  • Fever
  • Serositis (peritonitis, pleuritis, pericarditis)
  • Abdominal and/or chest pain
  • Arthralgias/arthritis
  • erysipelas-like skin lesions
5.3.2 Less Common Manifestations
  • Myalgias, pericarditis, scrotal swelling
  • Headaches, aseptic meningitis
5.3.3 Long-term Complications
  • Most concerning: secondary AA amyloidosis (cause of death)
  • Amyloid deposits in: GI tract, liver, spleen, heart, thyroid, testes
  • Most common in kidneys
  • Renal amyloidosis: proteinuria, nephrotic syndrome, or end-stage renal disease
  • GI manifestations typically malabsorptive
5.4 Diagnosis
  • Based on clinical criteria (Livneh criteria remain gold standard)
  • Genetic testing can be confirmatory
  • See diagnostic criteria table
5.5 Management Options
  • Mainstay: colchicine (prophylactic)
  • Prevents recurrence in majority
  • Prevents secondary amyloidosis development
  • Not effective during active flares
  • Response rates:
  • ~60% respond to daily colchicine alone
  • 20%–30% partial response → add adjunctive treatments
  • 5%–10% no response
5.5.1 Adjunctive Treatments for Partial/Non-responders
  • IL-1 inhibitors (most robust data)
  • Anti-TNF agents
  • Anti-IL-6 agents (small case series/reports)
6. Diagnostic Criteria Tables
6.1 FMF Diagnostic Criteria (Livneh et al.)
  • ≥2 major criteria; or
  • ≥1 minor criterion + ≥5 supportive criteria; or
  • 1 minor criterion + ≥4 of first 5 supportive criteria
Major Criteria (Typical Attacks)
  • Peritonitis (generalized)
  • Pleuritis (unilateral) or pericarditis
  • Monoarthritis (hip, knee, ankle)
  • Isolated fevers
Minor Criteria (Incomplete Attacks)
  • Abdomen, chest, joint attacks
  • Exertional leg pain
  • Positive response to colchicine
Supportive Criteria

1. Family history FMF

2. Appropriate ethnic origin

3. Age <20 at onset

4. Attacks severe requiring bedrest

5. Attacks spontaneously remit

6. Symptom-free between attacks

7. Transient elevations in WBC, ESR, SAA, fibrinogen

8. Episodic proteinuria/hematuria

9. Negative laparotomy/removal of healthy appendix

10. Parental consanguinity

6.2 Acute Hepatic Porphyria Overview Table
Type Enzyme (Gene) Prevalence Key Features
ADP ALA-dehydratase (ALAD) 8 reported cases Rarest; elevated ALA, normal PBG
AIP Hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS) ~1:20,000 Most common acute hepatic
HCP Coproporphyrinogen oxidase (CPOX) ~1:500,000 Both acute attacks + skin lesions
VP Protoporphyrinogen oxidase (PPOX) ~1:300,000 Both acute + skin; high in South Africa
7. Key Takeaways for Gastroenterologists
7.1 Why These Conditions Matter
  • Consequences of under-recognition:
  • Unnecessary surgery and testing
  • Repeated hospitalizations
  • Increasing attack frequency
  • Delays in effective treatment → disease progression/life-threatening complications
  • Wrong treatments with side effects
  • Death
7.2 When to Consider These Diagnoses
  • Consider when initial diagnostic workup has failed
  • Not part of initial differential → considered after common causes excluded
7.3 Diagnostic Approach Summary
Condition Key Diagnostic Test(s)
CAWP Carnett's sign, pinch test, TPI response
MALS CTA/MRA with deep expiration
CMI CTA (sensitivity 96%, specificity 94%)
SMVT CTA (sensitivity 93%, specificity 100%)
Food allergy Food-specific IgE blood/skin testing
MCAS Serum tryptase, 24-hr urine mast cell mediators
HAE Complement C4 test (low <50% normal)
AHP Single spot urine for ALA and PBG (>4× ULN diagnostic)
FMF Clinical criteria (Livneh), genetic testing confirmatory
7.4 Treatment Summary
Condition Primary Treatment
CAWP TPI (lidocaine ± steroid), severe → neurectomy
MALS Surgical division of MAL (open/laparoscopic)
CMI Endovascular or surgical revascularization
SMVT Systemic anticoagulation 3–6 months
Food allergy Strict avoidance + epinephrine
MCAS H1 antihistamines → mast cell stabilizers
HAE C1 inhibitor replacement or bradykinin antagonists (specialist)
AHP IV heme for attacks; givosiran for prevention
FMF Colchicine prophylaxis; IL-1 inhibitors if refractory
